interactions.summary: Jiang says he returned to China because his language and journalism skills gave him more opportunity there, America seemed stable and predictable, and he wanted a society in flux rather than a prewritten Yale-to-law-or-professor career path.
PBD asks whether Jiang publishes in China; Jiang says he refuses Chinese social media and Chinese interview requests, then PBD asks who reached out.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is Patrick Bet-David or the PBD side of the interview; SPEAKER_00 is Jiang except for brief intro/audio fragments. Diarization is treated as a hint and the exchange context controls attribution.

claims.claim: Jiang says America rewards talent and hard work more than most societies, while China and much of the world reward family power and connections.
He chose China because it was an open canvas of unpredictable change where he could learn and do things unavailable in a stable American career path.
He maintains a strict policy of not engaging Chinese social media, reporters, or offers to work with Chinese people because of compromise risk.

signature_moments.moment: China attracted Jiang because it was not predictable; it was a changing world where the script was not already written.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a world in which the world was unpredictable
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains his biography as a search for historical flux rather than simple pro-China sentiment.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang’s description of early-2000s China as a society in flux, full of change and opportunity compared with stable American career paths.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang recounts returning to China after deportation because he saw greater opportunity and historical change there.
